Name: spectral_stability_bounds_for_low_latency_inference
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Low-Latency Inference in Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks, we can develop a novel framework for low-latency inference in deep neural networks that optimizes the trade-off between accuracy and latency.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have focused on adaptive mixed-precision training and quantization techniques for efficient inference. However, these methods do not explicitly consider spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks, which are crucial for guaranteeing the reliability and robustness of low-latency inference. Our proposal distinguishes from existing work by incorporating these frameworks to develop a more comprehensive and theoretically-grounded approach.
Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved state-of-the-art performance in various applications, but their computational complexity and latency can be significant barriers to deployment in real-time systems. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that leverages spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to optimize the trade-off between accuracy and latency in deep neural networks. Our approach involves developing a spectral stability bound that characterizes the robustness of a neural network to perturbations, and using this bound to guide the optimization of the network's architecture and parameters. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments on various benchmark datasets and compare our results to state-of-the-art methods.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on several benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10, to evaluate the performance of our proposed framework. We will compare our results to state-of-the-art methods, including SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ, in terms of accuracy, latency, and computational complexity. We will also conduct ablation studies to investigate the impact of different components of our framework on the overall performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is the computational complexity of our proposed framework, which may be higher than existing methods due to the need to compute spectral stability bounds. Another limitation is the potential difficulty in optimizing the trade-off between accuracy and latency, which may require careful tuning of hyperparameters and architecture design.
